refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall achieve and maintain operational availability at or above the customer-defined target threshold over the agreed evaluation period.
refined_fr.rationale: The original FR conflates two distinct obligations: (1) meeting a performance target and (2) sustaining it over time. The refined version separates these concerns while remaining solution-neutral. It avoids prescribing how availability is achieved (e.g., redundancy, predictive maintenance, rapid repair) and focuses on the measurable outcome in the problem domain. The phrase 'achieve and maintain' captures both the initial attainment and sustained performance without introducing implementation details.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Collect operational telemetry (uptime/downtime logs, scheduled vs. unscheduled maintenance events) over the agreed evaluation period. Calculate availability as (Total Time − Downtime) / Total Time. Compare against customer-defined target (e.g., ≥0.97). Verify that the measured availability meets or exceeds the threshold for the full evaluation window.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-004.1: The NGHE solution shall achieve the customer-defined operational availability target at the start of the evaluation period.
FR-NGHE-004.2: The NGHE solution shall sustain operational availability at or above the customer-defined target throughout the agreed evaluation period.

candidate_dps: DP1: Implement redundant critical subsystems (power, control, sensing) with automatic failover logic to mask single-point failures and maintain service continuity.
DP2: Design modular architecture with hot-swappable components and standardized interfaces to enable rapid replacement of failed units without system shutdown.
DP3: Establish predictive maintenance framework using condition monitoring and degradation models to detect and address failures before they impact availability.
DP4: Implement graceful degradation strategy where non-critical functions are suspended to preserve core operational capability during partial failures.
DP5: Deploy distributed control architecture with local autonomy and asynchronous coordination to reduce single-point control failures and communication bottlenecks.
DP6: Establish comprehensive fault detection and isolation (FDI) system with automated diagnostics and operator alerting to minimize mean-time-to-repair (MTTR).
DP7: Implement configuration management and rapid reconfiguration capability to restore system to known-good state without full restart.
DP8: Design for testability with built-in self-test (BIST) and health-check mechanisms to continuously validate subsystem readiness.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP1 + DP3 + DP6 (integrated approach): Implement redundant critical subsystems with automatic failover (DP1) combined with predictive maintenance via condition monitoring (DP3) and comprehensive fault detection with rapid diagnostics (DP6). This three-pillar approach addresses availability through both reactive resilience (redundancy/failover) and proactive prevention (predictive maintenance) while minimizing MTTR through fast fault isolation.

potential_sources: DP1-DP3 coupling: Redundancy strategy must align with maintenance scheduling; predictive maintenance may defer component replacement, affecting spare-parts inventory for failover.
DP1-DP6 coupling: Failover logic depends on FDI accuracy; false positives in fault detection trigger unnecessary switchovers, degrading availability.
DP3-DP6 coupling: Condition monitoring data feeds diagnostics; data quality and latency directly impact MTTR effectiveness.
Implicit coupling to worksite safety plan: Failover and maintenance activities must not violate safety interlocks or operational policies; requires explicit coordination mechanism.
coupling_check.notes: Couplings are manageable through clear interface contracts (e.g., FDI output format, condition-monitoring data schema, maintenance scheduling rules). Recommend explicit decoupling via abstraction layers: (1) standardized fault/health status interface between DP1 and DP6, (2) maintenance-window negotiation protocol between DP3 and DP1, (3) safety-constraint validation layer between all DPs and worksite policies.

assumptions_risks: ASSUMPTION: Redundant subsystems can be procured, integrated, and maintained within cost and schedule constraints. RISK: Redundancy adds weight, power, and complexity; may violate physical or operational constraints not yet surfaced.

ASSUMPTION: Condition-monitoring sensors and data infrastructure are available and reliable. RISK: Monitoring system itself becomes a critical dependency; failure of monitoring degrades predictive maintenance effectiveness.

ASSUMPTION: Fault detection algorithms can achieve high sensitivity (detect real faults) and specificity (minimize false positives) in the operational environment. RISK: Noisy or ambiguous sensor data may trigger spurious failovers, reducing availability.

ASSUMPTION: Mean-time-to-repair (MTTR) can be reduced to acceptable levels through diagnostics and modular design. RISK: If field repair is constrained by logistics, weather, or safety protocols, MTTR may remain high despite good diagnostics.

ASSUMPTION: Worksite safety plan permits automated failover and maintenance activities without manual intervention delays. RISK: Safety interlocks or operator approval requirements may introduce latency that violates availability targets.

ASSUMPTION: Evaluation period is long enough (weeks to months) to observe representative failure modes and validate 0.97 availability claim. RISK: Short evaluation windows may not capture rare failure modes or seasonal variations.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Refine FR to explicitly address: (1) Definition of 'operational availability' (e.g., does it include planned maintenance windows? Does it account for partial degradation?), (2) Acceptable MTTR and MTBF targets that support 0.97 availability math, (3) Constraints on redundancy (cost, weight, power budget), (4) Safety and operational policy boundaries that may restrict failover or maintenance actions, (5) Evaluation methodology (continuous monitoring, statistical sampling, accelerated testing). Propose revised FR: 'The NGHE solution shall achieve and maintain operational availability ≥ 0.97 (measured as uptime excluding planned maintenance) over the agreed evaluation period, subject to [MTBF target], [MTTR limit], [redundancy constraints], and [safety policy compliance]. Availability shall be validated via [measurement method] with [confidence level].'

im_boundary.name: Safety Services
im_boundary.scope: Centralized module responsible for system health monitoring, fault detection, predictive maintenance, redundancy coordination, and failover orchestration across all critical subsystems.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Receives continuous sensor data, system state, and performance metrics from Vehicle and Teleoperation subsystems for condition monitoring and fault detection.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Emits fault notifications, failover triggers, maintenance alerts, and diagnostic summaries to Vehicle and Teleoperation for corrective action.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Receives operational task context to inform predictive maintenance scheduling and failover strategy; returns task suspension/resumption recommendations during fault recovery.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Communicates redundancy state, failover coordination signals, and diagnostic telemetry to remote monitoring and NGHE Solution for centralized oversight.

module_containment_scores.notes: Excellent fit: Safety Services is the natural owner of fault detection (DP6), predictive maintenance (DP3), and failover orchestration (DP1). All three DPs are cohesive within a health/resilience mandate. Minor diffusion: redundancy coordination may require Vehicle to manage physical switchover, but logic and diagnostics remain centralized.

Poor fit: Vehicle owns physical subsystems but not cross-system health logic. Redundancy/failover logic would diffuse across multiple vehicle components; predictive maintenance and fault detection would require duplication or tight coupling to Safety Services anyway. Creates ownership ambiguity.

Poor fit: Teleoperation is a control interface, not a health/resilience layer. Placing DP1+DP3+DP6 here would scatter fault detection and failover logic away from system-wide visibility. High diffusion to Vehicle and Safety Services.

Moderate fit: NGHE Solution could host high-level orchestration and predictive analytics, but real-time fault detection and failover must be local to minimize latency. Splits DP6 (diagnostics) and DP1 (failover) across remote and local boundaries, increasing coupling and MTTR.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: Safety Services is the single authoritative module for system resilience and health management. It owns the integrated logic for redundancy coordination (DP1), condition-based predictive maintenance (DP3), and comprehensive fault detection with rapid diagnostics (DP6). External modules (Vehicle, Teleoperation, NGHE Solution) consume SafetyAlert and Tasking outputs and provide Telemetry inputs, maintaining clean separation of concerns.
containment_risks: Risk: Redundancy failover may require Vehicle to execute physical switchover commands; Safety Services must not assume Vehicle can always respond in time. Mitigation: Define failover latency SLAs and pre-position failover logic in Vehicle as a thin executor, not a decision-maker.

Risk: Predictive maintenance (DP3) depends on accurate Telemetry; if sensor data is noisy or delayed, diagnostics (DP6) may produce false positives. Mitigation: Implement data validation and filtering within Safety Services; establish Telemetry quality contracts.

Risk: MTTR optimization (DP6 goal) requires rapid communication with Vehicle and Teleoperation; network latency could undermine fast fault isolation. Mitigation: Ensure SafetyAlert and Tasking interfaces have low-latency guarantees; consider local fault detection agents in Vehicle if needed.

Risk: Redundancy coordination (DP1) across multiple critical subsystems may exceed Safety Services' scope if subsystems are independently owned. Mitigation: Establish clear ownership of each redundant subsystem and define failover handoff protocols.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: No revision to DP choice needed. The integrated DP1+DP3+DP6 is well-contained within Safety Services. However, clarify that Safety Services owns the *logic and orchestration* of redundancy, predictive maintenance, and fault detection, while Vehicle and Teleoperation own the *execution* of failover commands and maintenance actions. This preserves cohesion in Safety Services while respecting module boundaries.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: High. Availability target (≥0.97) is measurable over the agreed evaluation period. Safety Services module can emit telemetry and SafetyAlert outputs that directly feed availability metrics (uptime, MTTR, failover success rate). Worksite safety plan and operational policies provide context for acceptable failure modes and maintenance windows.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Clear. Safety Services owns the integrated logic (DP1+DP3+DP6) with well-defined external interfaces (Telemetry in, SafetyAlert out, Tasking inout, Network Link inout). Vehicle and Teleoperation are consumers/executors, not decision-makers. NGHE Solution receives diagnostic telemetry for remote oversight. Ownership boundaries are explicit.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP1+DP3+DP6 (redundancy + predictive maintenance + fault detection) directly and sufficiently addresses the FR. Redundancy and failover (DP1) provide reactive resilience; predictive maintenance (DP3) and fault detection (DP6) provide proactive prevention and rapid MTTR. The three-pillar approach is logically sound and covers both availability drivers (uptime and recovery speed). No independence violations detected; the DPs are coupled by design (all feed into Safety Services orchestration).

diffusion_risks: DP1 (redundancy failover) diffusion: Physical switchover execution may scatter across Vehicle subsystems. Mitigation in place: Safety Services owns decision logic; Vehicle owns thin executor role. Verify failover latency SLAs are defined and Vehicle can meet them.

DP3 (predictive maintenance) diffusion: Depends on Telemetry quality from Vehicle and Teleoperation. If sensor data validation is weak, maintenance scheduling logic may degrade. Mitigation: Establish Telemetry quality contracts and implement data filtering within Safety Services.

DP6 (fault detection) diffusion: Real-time diagnostics require low-latency SafetyAlert delivery. Network latency to NGHE Solution could delay remote diagnostics. Mitigation: Ensure local fault detection is primary; NGHE Solution receives diagnostic telemetry asynchronously for post-incident analysis.

DP1+DP3+DP6 coordination diffusion: If critical subsystems are independently owned (e.g., propulsion, steering, comms), redundancy coordination may require cross-subsystem handoff protocols. Mitigation: Define clear failover ownership and handoff procedures per subsystem.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Excellent containment (0.92 score). Safety Services is the natural single authority for health/resilience logic. All three DPs are cohesive within a health monitoring and failover orchestration mandate. Minor diffusion is acceptable and mitigated: Vehicle executes failover commands (thin executor), not decision-making. Telemetry inputs and SafetyAlert outputs maintain clean separation of concerns. No duplication of fault detection or predictive maintenance logic across modules. Recommended revision is clarification only: Safety Services owns *logic and orchestration*; Vehicle/Teleoperation own *execution*. This preserves cohesion and respects module boundaries.

recommended_revisions: Clarify Safety Services ownership model: Safety Services is the authoritative owner of redundancy coordination logic (DP1), predictive maintenance scheduling (DP3), and fault detection/diagnostics (DP6). Vehicle and Teleoperation are executors of failover commands and maintenance actions, not decision-makers. Document this in the module interface specification.

Define failover latency SLAs: Specify maximum acceptable latency for SafetyAlert delivery to Vehicle and Teleoperation. Verify Vehicle can execute failover commands within this latency. If network latency is a risk, pre-position failover logic in Vehicle as a thin state machine (not a full decision engine).

Establish Telemetry quality contracts: Define sensor data freshness, accuracy, and completeness requirements for predictive maintenance (DP3) and fault detection (DP6). Implement data validation and filtering within Safety Services to reject noisy or stale telemetry.

Document redundancy coordination protocols: For each critical subsystem (propulsion, steering, comms, etc.), define which subsystem owns physical redundancy and how Safety Services orchestrates failover. Avoid ambiguity in handoff procedures.

Verify availability measurement: Confirm that Safety Services can emit availability metrics (uptime, MTTR, failover success rate) that directly map to the FR acceptance criterion (≥0.97 availability). Define the evaluation period and measurement methodology in the operational test plan.

decision.justification: All three gates (FR-DP, DP-IM, FR-IM) pass with high confidence, and the FR is sufficiently detailed for decomposition. However, the recommended_revisions identify critical clarifications needed at the current level before proceeding to sub-FR derivation. Specifically: (1) Safety Services ownership model must be explicitly documented to prevent diffusion of decision logic into Vehicle/Teleoperation executors; (2) failover latency SLAs are essential for availability verification and must be defined before sub-FRs can be properly scoped; (3) Telemetry quality contracts are prerequisites for DP3 and DP6 effectiveness. These are not gate failures but architectural clarifications that will prevent rework downstream. Given high safety criticality, proceeding to sub-FRs without these clarifications risks misalignment between Safety Services and Vehicle/Teleoperation on decision authority and latency constraints.

revise.why: The three DPs (DP1: redundancy+failover, DP3: predictive maintenance, DP6: fault detection) are logically sound but require explicit refinement of ownership boundaries and interface contracts before sub-FR derivation. Specifically: (1) Clarify DP1 as 'Safety Services orchestrates redundancy failover decisions; Vehicle executes failover commands within defined latency SLA'; (2) Clarify DP3 as 'Safety Services schedules predictive maintenance based on Telemetry inputs meeting defined quality contracts'; (3) Clarify DP6 as 'Safety Services detects faults and emits SafetyAlert; NGHE Solution receives diagnostic telemetry asynchronously for post-incident analysis'. These refinements will establish the decision/execution boundary and latency assumptions needed for sub-FR decomposition.

assumptions: Safety Services is the single authoritative owner of redundancy coordination, predictive maintenance scheduling, and fault detection logic (not distributed across Vehicle/Teleoperation).
Vehicle and Teleoperation are thin executors of failover commands and maintenance actions, not decision-makers.
Failover latency from SafetyAlert emission to Vehicle command execution is acceptable for the ≥0.97 availability target (to be quantified in revised DP).
Telemetry from Vehicle and Teleoperation meets defined quality contracts (freshness, accuracy, completeness) sufficient for predictive maintenance and fault detection.
Network latency to NGHE Solution does not block local fault detection; remote diagnostics are asynchronous and post-incident.
Critical subsystems (propulsion, steering, comms) have defined redundancy ownership and failover handoff procedures (to be documented in revised DP).
open_risks: Failover latency SLA not yet quantified: If Vehicle cannot execute failover commands within acceptable latency, pre-positioned failover logic in Vehicle may be required, increasing complexity and diffusion risk.
Telemetry quality contracts not yet established: Weak sensor data validation could degrade predictive maintenance and fault detection effectiveness, reducing actual availability below target.

Cross-subsystem redundancy coordination ambiguity: If propulsion, steering, and comms redundancy are independently owned, Safety Services orchestration may require complex handoff protocols; unclear ownership could lead to failover deadlock.
NGHE Solution diagnostic latency: Remote diagnostics may not support real-time root cause analysis if network latency is high; post-incident analysis may be insufficient for rapid MTTR.
Safety Services module scalability: If Telemetry volume or failover frequency is high, Safety Services may become a bottleneck; load distribution strategy not yet defined.
evidence_needed: Failover latency SLA: Define maximum acceptable latency (e.g., <100ms) from SafetyAlert emission to Vehicle command execution. Verify Vehicle architecture can meet this SLA.

Telemetry quality contracts: Define sensor data freshness (e.g., <1s), accuracy (e.g., ±5%), and completeness (e.g., >99% availability) requirements. Implement data validation and filtering logic in Safety Services.

Redundancy coordination protocols: For each critical subsystem, document which subsystem owns physical redundancy, how Safety Services orchestrates failover, and handoff procedures. Verify no ambiguity or deadlock scenarios.

Availability measurement methodology: Confirm Safety Services can emit uptime, MTTR, and failover success rate metrics. Define evaluation period (e.g., 12 months of operational data) and acceptance criteria (≥0.97 availability).
Network latency analysis: Measure latency from Safety Services to Vehicle, Teleoperation, and NGHE Solution. Verify local fault detection is primary and remote diagnostics are asynchronous.

Safety Services ownership model documentation: Update module interface specification to explicitly state Safety Services owns decision logic; Vehicle/Teleoperation own execution. Include decision/execution boundary diagram.
